metadata.title: How to Do "Deep" Investment Research: Process, Key Points and Pitfalls
metadata.guest_role: Practical Investments Host, Buy-Side Research Lead

conclusion: Yongqing maps deep buy-side research into a repeatable SOP — know your money, business, and market before any ticker. Idea funnel runs from base-territory coverage through screening, ~1.5M-character public dossiers, 360° field work, and disciplined tracking; AI accelerates drafts but garbage-in PR still fails. For investors seeking 10–15% on three-year capital: Watch TSM and NVDA as 'simple-body' AI-chain exemplars, size on process not single-quarter luck.
background: Practical Investments ep.188 (June 21, 2026) is a Wu Xiaobo Channel live replay where Yongqing teaches how to do deep investment research — process, pitfalls, and information sources. He frames good investments as self-knowledge plus business plus market understanding, then walks a full buy-side SOP: idea sourcing (prior coverage, bottleneck chains, 13F opereye, peers), fast screening, deep coverage modules (industry history, unit economics, management, rivals, financials, market/valuation), field research, and ongoing tracking. Emphasis: rule-based decisions beat lottery buys on one headline quarter; AI helps 70–80% of prep if inputs are clean.
important_facts: Three-lens framework: money attributes (3+ year capital, ~10–15% target), business quality (three value styles — growth, quality-at-fair-price, deep value — session focuses on quality), and market context (sector rotation, valuation regimes). Good research SOP: idea → screen → deep cover → verify via research → track anomalies — like clearing a game map before fighting.

Deep-cover checklist: industry/company history, business and unit economics, management and cap structure, competitor count (prefer ≤2–3 strong players), financial forensics, market narrative and valuation, then distill to 2–3 tracking questions. Quality single-name work ≈1.5M characters of public material plus expert interviews; skip names with fraud flags, chronic decline, or serial pivoting.

Information hygiene: filter PR podcasts, require buyer-side voices with skin in the game, cross-check via industry veterans and investor 360° (long, watchlist, sellers, shorts). AI bottleneck chain example — GPU → optics → HBM storage → CPU → advanced packaging — shows simple vs hard problems; ~30 of 80 tracked US managers bought TSM in Q1 as a 'simple body' signal.

mental_model.name: Self × Business × Market × SOP Discipline
mental_model.components: Edge is not random tips — it is process: map the industry, know why you can win, track only what moves the thesis. Simple bodies (dominant incumbents in rising chains) beat heroic ten-baggers for most capital. Adding size after lucky gains without framework is how accounts bleed.
mental_model.application: Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM): Watch as AI-chain simple body — bottleneck diffusion and fund-manager clustering signal durable relevance; apply Yongqing SOP before sizing. NVIDIA (NVDA): Watch as first-link compute landlord — pair process discipline with chain-stage awareness, not headline-quarter bets.

key_insights.view: Process beats lottery even when luck pays once.
key_insights.question: Why is buying on one great quarter dangerous?
key_insights.answer: Yongqing says a single strong print inside no prior map is still gambling — you may win but the workflow is wrong. Institutions log idea source, screen rationale, and cover conclusions so luck does not scale into oversized adds after +30% runs.

key_insights.view: Simple bodies first; hard problems optional.
key_insights.question: How should retail handle AI semis?
key_insights.answer: In chip cycles, NVDA/ASML/TSM were 'simple' before storage became the hard HBM bottleneck. Master one or two names with full SOP — ~10× PE, ~10% growth, ~5% yield ideal — rather than twenty 5% bets you do not understand.

key_insights.view: AI is research staff, not research substitute.
key_insights.question: Where does AI fit the SOP?
key_insights.answer: Models hit ~75–85% on financial frameworks and reading plans if you feed primary sources — not PR. Build curated info libraries, simulate great investors' question sets, then use humans for field checks and edge identification.

top_investment_implications.thesis: Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM): Yongqing's quality-compounder lane (~10% growth plus dividend) — simple-body winner when full SOP clears valuation; process-led sizing, not headline momentum
NVIDIA (NVDA): Semis simple-body leader in Yongqing's framework — Long only when cycle thesis and valuation pass the full research funnel, not chatbot impulse

golden_quotes: Research is like clearing a game map — you walk it piece by piece before you fight.
Buying on one great quarter without a map still loses more often than it wins.
Nail the simple problems first; you do not need a ten-bagger in an industry you barely know.
